summary: I was born in Oregon, where my family has deep pioneer roots on the West Coast. Growing up there, I was influenced by my mother's strong sense of spatial design, which shaped my early artistic interests. Oregon was the starting point of my journey into art and creativity.

At Mills College, I had the incredible opportunity to study with Alexander Archipenko, which was a pivotal moment in my artistic development. The education I received there helped me refine my skills and deepen my understanding of modern sculpture. Mills College was truly foundational in shaping my career.

Alexander Archipenko was my teacher at Mills College, and studying under him was a turning point for me. His approach to sculpture opened new perspectives and techniques that influenced my work profoundly. Learning from him helped me embrace modernism and experiment with form.

I taught at the Anna Head School in Berkeley, which was a very rewarding experience. Teaching there helped me find inner strength and develop my own philosophy of teaching art. It was a meaningful way to connect with young artists and nurture their creativity.

I wrote for Arts and Architecture before leaving San Francisco for Europe in 1950. Contributing to that publication allowed me to engage with the art community and share my ideas. It was part of my transition from teaching to immersing myself more fully in the art world.

I moved to Europe in 1950, intending to stay six weeks but ended up living there for thirteen years. This period was crucial for my growth as I immersed myself in a broader cultural context and learned from major figures in modern sculpture. Europe was where I experimented with new materials and techniques, expanding beyond traditional wood and clay.

During my time in Europe, I lived in a small studio in Saint-Germain-des-Prés in Paris. This vibrant neighborhood was a hub for artists and intellectuals, and it provided a stimulating environment for my creative work. It was there that I met influential artists like Henry Moore and Constantin Brâncuși.

Paris was my home base in Europe, especially in the Saint-Germain-des-Prés area, where I lived and worked in the 1950s. The city’s rich artistic culture and history deeply influenced my work. Meeting artists such as Henry Moore and Constantin Brâncuși there was invaluable to my development.

I had the privilege of meeting and working with Henry Moore while living in Paris. His approach to sculpture inspired me to explore new forms and materials. Interacting with him was part of the intense growth and experimentation I experienced in Europe.

Constantin Brâncuși was another major figure I met in Paris who influenced my artistic journey. His innovative sculptural style encouraged me to push boundaries in my own work. Being around him during my European years was a profound experience.

In 1954, I created a floor-to-ceiling sculpture for the Galleria Spazio in Rome. This commission was significant as it marked one of my first major public works in Europe. It allowed me to showcase my evolving style and techniques to a broader audience.

Rome was an important city for my career in the mid-1950s. In 1954, I completed a major sculpture for the Galleria Spazio there. The city’s rich artistic heritage inspired me as I developed new concepts like my 'sign and ensemble' idea.

In 1957, I was commissioned to create a set of gates for Princess Pignatelli’s grotto in Santa Marinella. This project introduced my 'sign and ensemble' concept, which was a breakthrough in my work. Collaborating with her was a highlight of my European period.

Santa Marinella was the location of a significant commission I completed in 1957 for Princess Pignatelli’s grotto. Creating gates there allowed me to develop and showcase my 'sign and ensemble' concept. The place holds an important place in my artistic history.

Peggy Guggenheim commissioned me to create gates for her palazzo in Venice after I led an Italian art tour in 1960. This was a prestigious project that connected me with one of the most influential art patrons of the time. Working for her was a major milestone in my career.

Venice became an important city for me in 1960 when Peggy Guggenheim commissioned gates for her palazzo there. The city’s unique atmosphere and art scene enriched my work. Venice was also the place where I led an Italian art tour, further deepening my connections in the art world.

Esther Robles invited me to return to Los Angeles in 1961 to create a fountain. Her invitation was the reason I came back to America after thirteen years in Europe. Since then, she has been a key figure in supporting my work in Southern California.

Los Angeles became my home again in 1961 after Esther Robles invited me to create a fountain there. Since returning, I have completed many sculptural commissions throughout Southern California. The city has been central to my later career and artistic output.

entity: California Federal Savings and Loan Building
summary: In 1965, I designed 'Structure and Flow, No. 2' for the California Federal Savings and Loan Building on Wilshire Boulevard. This was one of my major public commissions in Los Angeles and helped establish my reputation locally. The project was a significant milestone in my American career.

Wilshire Boulevard was the site of my 1965 commission for the California Federal Savings and Loan Building. Creating 'Structure and Flow, No. 2' there was a key moment in my work in Los Angeles. The location gave my sculpture high visibility in the city.
cues: California Federal Savings and Loan Building

summary: I created the 'Game Wall' sculpture for California State University, Fullerton, as part of my growing body of public commissions in Southern California. This work allowed me to engage with the academic community and contribute to the campus environment. It was an important project following my return from Europe.

At California State University, Long Beach, I created 'U as a Set,' another significant public sculpture. This commission further established my presence in the Southern California art scene. It was part of a series of works I completed for educational institutions after returning from Europe.

In 1966, I created 'Three Fires' for the Fresno Mall, which was my first work produced from my Venice studio in Los Angeles. This project marked my expansion into public art beyond the immediate Los Angeles area. Fresno was an important location in my California commissions.

Venice, Los Angeles, became my studio location after returning from Europe. From there, I created works like 'Three Fires' for Fresno and other commissions throughout Southern California. Venice has been a creative base for much of my later career.

My most ambitious project was the exterior sculpture at St. Basil's Catholic Church, completed between 1966 and 1969. This included a rectory screen, eight screenlike entrance doors, and fifteen three-dimensional stained glass windows. It was a complex and rewarding commission that showcased my ability to work on a large scale.
